story:
Obsession sneaks in, then roots itself. I didn’t go to med school or take a job as a police coroner’s assistant to become a monster. If there’s a beginning, it’s Dr. Richard Lackington’s lecture: “What is life?” he asked, cane tapping under Da Vinci’s Vitruvian Man. “Life is chemical. It is electric.” He called it the Frankenstein Theory—the idea that with the right ratios of chemicals and electricity, you could spark life, maybe even from death. He joked that women already do this, cell by cell. We laughed. I listened.

But the seed sprouted in Ethics class. Our final paper: Life Begins When? The Role of Philosophy and Rhetoric in Medical Ethics. I argued that medicine always plays God—sliding scales of power, decisions that start or stop hearts, cells that mean one thing to faith and another to a lab. I wrote about a 1980s case where fetal tissue went to research, about how rhetoric can turn a clump of cells into a moral cliff. I got a B. “Understood but did not appreciate,” my professor wrote. I heard: You’re thinking like a scientist, not a student.

At the morgue, I learned death by its mechanisms. My hands opened torsos, traced the bullet’s path, lifted knife tips from bone. I improved my sutures without worrying about blood loss. In debates I pushed for compulsory organ donation: If I’m gone and my liver is good, take it. Why should wealth jump the line? Playing Devil’s Advocate became easy. In neurosurgery, I reduced the brain to what it is: mostly fat and protein, suspended in water, pulsing electricity. When it’s broken down like that, life looks simple. If it’s just slime and sparks, couldn’t you remake it?

That was the lie I started telling myself. I tell you now it’s a lie because none of what follows is legal, ethical, or likely to work in any world where safety matters. But obsession doesn’t negotiate. It whispers: It’s easy to steal from a police morgue if you’re careful. No one looks twice at thermoses when you’re the one trusted to keep things sterile. Organs get flushed with cold saline, bagged, iced. Compatibility? You have the charts and tests. Timelines? Rig a rough “life support” of pumps and oxygenation. Bodies are harder, so you learn bones before flesh—an arm from a junkyard, a foot from the desert. You convince yourself you’re assembling more than parts. You are not; you are crossing lines.

Sometimes a man like Percy finds you, someone convinced a limb isn’t his. He wants it gone. In real medicine you’d treat the mind first; you’d never cut. I didn’t follow real medicine. I didn’t take the whole leg; I only mangled the lower part badly enough that he later lost it in a hospital—damage I caused, consequences I pretended not to own. He thanked me. He offered to share the flesh. I stopped there. I told myself I still had lines. But my line had already been crossed.

There were easier ways to make life: I have a uterus. I could join a cloning lab. But that would prove nothing. I wanted to prove the Frankenstein Theory, to be smarter than Victor, smarter than Lackington and his theater. I told myself progress demands sacrifice, that I could quit anytime. I repeated I could stop until the words lost meaning.

I haven’t made a creature. I’ve made a choice. I will show how easily we pretend to be gods with the pieces the dead leave behind. That is the danger: not that life is simple, but that I convinced myself it was. I am become Frankenstein, and obsession is the current that keeps me moving.
